text: The surgeon begins the procedure by using robotic instruments to dissect adhesions, creating a suitable working space for the prostatectomy.
The surgeon performs cold cut dissection to carefully separate tissue, minimizing the risk of thermal damage to the intestine.
The surgical procedure utilizes three robotic arms and a robot-controlled camera to assist in the prostatectomy.
The assistant operates a robotic workstation to apply suction and traction within the surgical field, using staples as necessary to secure tissue.
The surgeon uses scissors with monopolar energy in the right hand to perform precise dissection during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses tweezers with bipolar energy in the left hand, while the Prograsp clamp in the third robotic arm assists with manipulation and counter-attraction during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon performs a transperitoneal dissection to address the seminal vesicles, utilizing the approach for precise visualization and separation of these structures.
The surgeon grips the arteries supplying the seminal vesicles to minimize cautery use, preventing potential damage to the nearby neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral face of the seminal vesicles, aiming to preserve the neurovascular structures visible in the area to maintain erectile function.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the seminal vesicles and makes an incision in the fascia of Lennon-Billiers, opening access to the perirectal space. Cold cut dissection is used to carefully separate the prostate from the rectum, minimizing the risk of rectal injury.

The surgeon performs dissection of the anterior wall by pulling the middle umbilical ligament, using scissors with monopolar energy. The mesh is observed to be close to the dissection area, potentially hindering progress. The surgeon remains in the lax tissue until the pubis is visualized, confirming the dissection plane is adequate and minimizing risk to vascular and adjacent structures.

The surgeon identifies the endopelvic fascia and begins dissecting the periprostatic fat, performing the dissection subtly with minimal bleeding. The removal of this fat aids in the visualization of surrounding tissues and facilitates the identification of bleeding sources later in the procedure.

The robotic-assisted dissection focuses on the lifting muscles and the dorsal venous complex, preserving a significant amount of muscle fibers. Cold dissection is used to minimize damage to muscle fibers and nerves. The surgeon cuts the puboprostatic ligaments on the left side, enhancing exposure of the dorsal venous complex.
The surgeon uses robotic needle holders to ligate the dorsal venous complex with a zero PDS suture, employing a running knot technique for hermetic closure to minimize bleeding.
The surgeon navigates around the neurovascular bundles near the midline, carefully avoiding their inclusion in the suture while ligating the venous complex.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the vesicle neck, preparing to introduce electrodes from the Propep system to monitor nerve activity during the procedure. The robotic system is utilized for precise integration of the monitoring equipment.

The surgeon performs dissection of the vesicle neck, a challenging step due to unclear dissection planes. The balloon of the probe is filled and manipulated back and forth to help identify the interface between the prostate and bladder.
The surgeon opens the vesicle neck and minimizes traction to prevent expansion of the vesicle neck during the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon maintains a small vesicle neck size to favor continence and reduce urinary escape. During the posterior dissection of the vesicle neck, the surgeon navigates through a flat tissue plane, identifying the seminal vesicles while minimizing the risk of damage to the trigonum and ureteral meatus.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the posterior vesicle neck, revealing the white and shiny surface of the seminal vesicles in the background. The focus shifts to the posterior pedicles of the prostate.

The surgeon initiates neuropreservation by performing anterograde and retrograde dissection of the neurovascular bundles, carefully reducing tension on the nerves to prevent neuropraxia, which can lead to impotence.

The surgeon performs dissection on the neurovascular bundles, maintaining a high position using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, occasionally employing cautery. Nerve monitoring is utilized to confirm the correct dissection plane and ensure proper release of the neurovascular structures.
The surgeon approaches the apex of the prostate, applying cold irrigation to the surgical field to mitigate heat generated by cautery use and reduce potential nerve damage.
The surgeon performs an extra posterior dissection in the area where the Denonvilliers fascia has been opened, revealing the neurovascular bundles at this level.
With the neurovascular axes released, the surgeon applies hemolog grafts on the vascular pellicle to achieve early control of bleeding, enhancing visibility for continued dissection.

The surgeon continues the dissection using cautery with precision, applying additional grafts to control venous bleeding. Abundant irrigation is used to minimize heat transmission during the procedure.

The surgeon performs dissection and release of the neurovascular axes on the right side, following the same technique as previously described. The right posterior pedicle is also dissected using similar principles, ensuring careful handling of the structures.

The surgeon completes the apical dissection using cutting and cut-off techniques in the region of the previously placed ligature. The bipolar clamp is used to tighten the tissue, distinguishing it from the prostate. Lax tissue is visible as the surgeon enters the space where the urethra is located. Additional traction is applied to the prostate to enhance visualization of the urethra. The neurovascular bundles are visible in the lower part of the dissection area. The urethra is identified, and efforts are made to preserve as much urethral muscle as possible to maintain its continuity.

After the prostate is removed, the neurovascular bundles are visible on each side of the former prostate location. The urethra is seen with the bundles converging at the midline. Nerve monitoring is used to assess preservation, with muscle contraction observed upon nerve stimulation, and a corresponding action potential displayed on a separate screen.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the bladder and the urethra using two monocryl sutures of different colors, applying the Van Veldhoven technique. The sutures are placed with precision to avoid damaging the urethra or adjacent structures.
The surgeon performs a continuous anastomosis between the bladder and urethra, minimizing tissue manipulation and reducing the risk of urinary leakage.

The surgeon fills the bladder with at least 120 milliliters of solution to confirm the absence of leakage at the end of the anastomosis. Any identified leaks are addressed to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis, reducing the risk of streaks or vesicular neck sclerosis and promoting proper healing of the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon reduces the pneumoperitoneum to check for bleeding and examines the Douglas bag to ensure there are no liquid collections or unnoticed intestinal injuries.
